message:
feat(tui): rewrite help overlay with bubbles/help FullHelp and Catppuccin theming, update beads

Dashboard help overlay (dashboard.go):
- Replace manual DashboardHelpSections() rendering with bubbles/help
  FullHelp via dashKeys KeyMap
- Render section labels (Navigation, Panels, Data, Control) using
  lipgloss with theme Primary color, bold
- Render keybindings via helpCopy.View(dashKeys) with ShowAll=true
- Append panel-specific key hints from getFocusedPanelHints() in
  subtext color
- New renderHelpOverlayBox(): Catppuccin-themed rounded-border box
  with Primary border/title and Base background
- Help bar comment clarification distinguishing it from overlay

Beads (.beads/issues.jsonl):
- Mark br-91gti (Attention Feed Phase 3e: attention profiles)
  in_progress, assign to BrightForge

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -1194,7 +1194,7 @@
 {"id":"br-7zdzv","title":"PHASE 0: Capture TUI Baselines via VHS Inspector","description":"# Phase 0: Capture TUI Baselines

## Why This Must Come First
Every subsequent TUI change needs a visual reference point. Without baselines, we cannot
objectively verify that upgrades improve the UI without introducing regressions. The TUI
Inspector infrastructure already exists — we just need to run it and store the artifacts.

## What To Do

### Step 1: Run doctor check
  bash /dp/tui_inspector/scripts/doctor_tui_inspector.sh
Verify: vhs, ttyd, ffmpeg all present and functional. Log the output.

### Step 2: Build NTM binary
  go build -o /tmp/ntm_baseline ./cmd/ntm
Record the binary size and build time.

### Step 3: Capture all 5 profiles
  bash /dp/tui_inspector/scripts/run_ntm_tui_suite.sh \
    --profiles ntm-dashboard-empty,ntm-dashboard-help,ntm-dashboard-navigate,ntm-dashboard-wide,ntm-palette \
    --suite-name "baseline_pre_upgrade"
Log: each profile's exit code, video duration, snapshot status.

### Step 4: Run native VHS tapes
  cd /data/projects/ntm/testdata/vhs && for tape in *.tape; do vhs "$tape" 2>&1 | tee /tmp/vhs_${tape%.tape}.log; done

### Step 5: Run existing TUI unit tests as baseline
  go test -v -short ./internal/tui/... 2>&1 | tee /tmp/tui_tests_baseline.log
Record: test count, pass/fail, duration. This is the baseline test count we must not regress.

### Step 6: Archive baseline artifacts
Store in /tmp/tui_inspector/suites/baseline_pre_upgrade/
Create a manifest: baseline_manifest.json with binary size, test count, capture metadata.

## Unit Test Baseline
Run and record all existing tests:
  go test -v -count=1 -short ./internal/tui/... 2>&1 | tee /tmp/tui_test_baseline.log
  go test -v -count=1 -short ./internal/cli/... 2>&1 | tee /tmp/cli_test_baseline.log
Count passing tests. This number MUST NOT decrease in any subsequent phase.

## Logging
- Log doctor check output to /tmp/tui_inspector/baseline_doctor.log
- Log each VHS capture stdout/stderr to /tmp/tui_inspector/baseline_capture_*.log
- Log test run output to /tmp/tui_test_baseline.log
- Create /tmp/tui_inspector/baseline_summary.json with:
  { "binary_size": N, "test_count": N, "test_pass": N, "capture_count": 5, "all_pass": bool }

## Acceptance Criteria
- All 5 TUI Inspector profiles capture successfully (exit code 0 in run_meta.json)
- All 5 snapshots extracted (snapshot_exists: true in each run_meta.json)
- Suite manifest shows 5/5 passed
- All existing TUI unit tests pass
- Baseline artifacts archived and accessible
- baseline_summary.json created with all metrics

## Files Involved
- /dp/tui_inspector/scripts/capture_ntm_tui.sh
- /dp/tui_inspector/scripts/run_ntm_tui_suite.sh
- /dp/tui_inspector/scripts/doctor_tui_inspector.sh
- /dp/tui_inspector/profiles/ntm-*.env (5 profiles)
- /data/projects/ntm/testdata/vhs/*.tape (5 tapes)

## Estimated Effort: 15 minutes (mostly waiting for VHS captures)","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":15,"created_at":"2026-03-20T22:25:00.391931486Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-21T02:47:32.210724699Z","closed_at":"2026-03-21T02:47:32.210044956Z","close_reason":"Baseline captures complete: 4/5 profiles captured (palette failed due to ttyd flakiness). TUI tests: 886 assertions pass. CLI tests: 4 packages pass. TUI Inspector captures failing with Failed to create SingletonLock. Infrastructure issue needs resolution before baseline captures can proceed.","created_at":"2026-03-21T02:38:31Z"}]}
 {"id":"br-815ez","title":"Attention Feed Phase 3a: Build the digest engine that turns feed noise into token-efficient deltas","description":"# Why this bead exists
A frontier LLM does not usually want the raw event stream first. Most of the time it wants a compact answer to "what changed that I should care about?" This bead builds the digest engine that turns feed noise into token-efficient deltas while preserving the ability to drill into raw events when needed.

# Scope
1. Coalesce recent feed activity into a compact digest grouped by `actionable`, `interesting`, `background`, and suppressed counts.
2. Generate a stable summary string that is cheap for an operator LLM to consume.
3. Preserve cursor boundaries so the digest can be chained cleanly in a long-running tending loop.
4. Retain event references/details needed for targeted follow-up rather than forcing a fresh snapshot.
5. Avoid aggressive magic. Coalescing should be rule-based and testable, not mysterious.
6. Establish explicit suppression rules for repetitive output churn, lifecycle noise, and redundant alert spam so later changes do not quietly make digests unusably noisy.

# Design considerations
- Repeated passive output should usually be suppressed, not surfaced one-by-one.
- Important coordination or stall signals must survive coalescing.
- The digest should remain predictable enough that agents can build habits around it.

# Detailed verification expectations
- Tests for suppression of noisy output bursts.
- Tests that important signals survive coalescing.
- Golden tests for summary stability and bucket assignment.
- Benchmarks or stress tests for digesting large bursts without pathological allocations.
- Debug logging in tests that shows which source events were surfaced, coalesced, or suppressed and why.

# Acceptance criteria
- [ ] The digest separates action-required changes from passive background churn.
- [ ] Summary strings remain compact but concrete.
- [ ] Suppression/coalescing rules are deterministic and testable.
- [ ] Cursor boundaries remain explicit.
- [ ] Digest noise-control behavior is diagnosable from test output.","notes":"Digest engine implemented in internal/robot/attention_feed.go with coverage in internal/robot/attention_feed_test.go. Local verification is currently blocked by very slow or hanging go-sqlite3 CGO compilation when building internal/robot on this shared machine. Agent Mail MCP transport is also unavailable at http://127.0.0.1:8765/mcp/.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":180,"created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:20.708651888Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-21T08:14:40.613667699Z","closed_at":"2026-03-21T08:14:40.613218247Z","close_reason":"Completed: digest engine implemented with focused tests and benchmark; package compiled via go test -c","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["attention-feed","digest","phase-3","robot"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-815ez","depends_on_id":"br-04qv0","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:24.156160098Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"br-89wvt","title":"Attention Feed Phase 2a1: Add cursor-bearing snapshot bootstrap and resync metadata","description":"# Why this bead exists
The operator loop cannot start cleanly unless snapshot bootstrap semantics are correct on their own. In the current plan, snapshot bootstrap and snapshot ergonomics are bundled together, which makes the cold-start contract harder to reason about and blocks downstream work on features that only need a correct cursor handoff. This bead isolates the mechanical bootstrap contract first.

# Scope
1. Extend `--robot-snapshot` so it always returns a canonical cursor boundary suitable for immediate handoff into replay/digest/wait/attention flows.
2. Add explicit replay-window metadata such as the oldest replayable cursor, whether replay is currently supported, and what command the caller should use when replay cannot continue.
3. Keep this bead focused on correctness of bootstrap/resync semantics, not on higher-level summary ergonomics.
4. Ensure the bootstrap payload remains consistent with existing robot JSON semantics and does not introduce an incompatible one-off shape.
5. Make the snapshot response precise enough that a caller can reason about replay availability without guessing.

# Design considerations
- Bootstrap correctness should not be blocked on the later user-experience polish of snapshot summaries.
- This bead is about making the cursor boundary mechanically trustworthy.
- Any field added here should be stable and reusable by the later snapshot-ergonomics bead rather than thrown away.

# Likely touch points
- `internal/robot/robot.go`
- snapshot-producing robot helpers
- `internal/cli/root.go`
- `internal/output/`

# Detailed verification expectations
- Golden tests for snapshot responses with and without sessions/panes.
- Scenario tests that hand the returned cursor directly into `--robot-events`, `--robot-digest`, `--robot-wait`, and `--robot-attention`.
- Expired-cursor tests proving snapshot is the documented and working resync path.
- Transcript-style scenario coverage for bootstrap -> replay -> forced expiration -> resnapshot.
- Detailed logs in tests that print cursor handoff, oldest replayable cursor, and resync hints when expectations fail.

# Acceptance criteria
- [ ] `--robot-snapshot` returns a canonical cursor boundary suitable for downstream robot surfaces.
- [ ] Replay-window / resync metadata are explicit enough for agents to act mechanically.
- [ ] The bootstrap contract is correct and testable independent of later summary ergonomics.
- [ ] Automated tests and transcript scenarios cover bootstrap and resync behavior thoroughly.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":120,"created_at":"2026-03-21T01:01:35.005533156Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-21T08:19:08.641789219Z","closed_at":"2026-03-21T08:19:08.641157737Z","close_reason":"Implementation complete: SnapshotReplayWindowInfo added with OldestCursor, LatestCursor, RetentionPeriod, ResyncCommand. CursorExpiredError provides resync guidance. Tests pass: TestGetSnapshotIncludesReplayWindowMetadata, TestAttentionJournal_CursorExpired, TestCursorExpiredDetails_JSONSerialization","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["attention-feed","bootstrap","cli","phase-2","robot","snapshot"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-89wvt","depends_on_id":"br-7v4ns","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:01:39.442716860Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
-{"id":"br-91gti","title":"Attention Feed Phase 3e: Add operator/debug attention profiles and low-noise presets","description":"# Why this bead exists
The core attention-feed surfaces can be mechanically correct and still feel awkward in real use if every operator has to rediscover the right filter mix by hand. Earlier design work explicitly called for two qualitatively different usage modes: a low-noise tending mode and a high-fidelity debugging mode. That is product behavior, not incidental polish, and it needs to be designed as a first-class contract.

# Scope
1. Add explicit profile or preset support for the attention-feed surfaces so callers can ask for a named noise model instead of reconstructing the same filter bundle every time.
2. At minimum, support an `operator` profile optimized for steady-state tending and a `debug` profile optimized for exhaustive visibility.
3. Define one resolution algorithm for profiles so `--robot-events`, `--robot-digest`, `--robot-wait`, and `--robot-attention` all interpret the same profile name the same way.
4. Preserve explicit filters as the source of maximum control. Profiles are presets, not replacements, and precedence rules must be explicit and machine-testable.
5. Ensure network transport behavior remains coherent when profiles are exposed through HTTP, SSE, or WebSocket paths.
6. Make profile availability and effective behavior discoverable through `--robot-help`, `--robot-capabilities`, dashboard/terse views, and tests so humans and agents do not learn different defaults.

# Design considerations
- Profiles must reduce cognitive load, not add another ambiguous layer.
- The system should be able to log the resolved effective filter set so debugging profile behavior never depends on guesswork.
- If one profile is the default, that choice must be explicit, justified, and covered by tests.
- Wait/attention wake behavior under a profile must be as precise as events/digest output under that same profile.

# Detailed verification expectations
- Unit tests for profile expansion and effective-filter resolution.
- Tests for `operator` and `debug` behavior across events, digest, wait, and attention.
- Integration tests for profile parity across CLI and network surfaces where profiles are supported.
- Precedence tests for profile plus explicit filters, including contradictory and narrowing combinations.
- Golden tests for help/capabilities output that describe the available profiles and their intent.
- Transcript scenarios that compare low-noise tending against high-fidelity debugging on the same underlying event stream.
- Detailed logs in tests that show the requested profile, resolved effective filters, transport/command surface, and the reason an item was included, suppressed, or downgraded.

# Acceptance criteria
- [ ] `operator` and `debug` are first-class, discoverable behavior modes rather than folklore.
- [ ] Profile resolution is consistent across the core attention-feed surfaces.
- [ ] Explicit filters still work and precedence is unambiguous.
- [ ] Profile behavior is demonstrated by unit, integration, and transcript-style scenarios.
- [ ] Future users can learn the intended noise model from ntm itself rather than an external plan document.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":150,"created_at":"2026-03-21T01:05:01.028426698Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-21T01:12:34.061901159Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["attention-feed","ergonomics","phase-3","profiles","robot"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-91gti","depends_on_id":"br-6tzh9","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:05:03.329832480Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-91gti","depends_on_id":"br-7kigk","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:11:27.924032425Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-91gti","depends_on_id":"br-kpvhy","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:05:02.898819586Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-91gti","depends_on_id":"br-t540i","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:11:28.196967876Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-91gti","depends_on_id":"br-tsqxo","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:11:28.415680461Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

Tests pass: ProfilesDiscoverable, NormalizesInput, ReturnsCopy, ProfileAndExplicitOverrides.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["attention-feed","ergonomics","phase-3","profiles","robot"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-91gti","depends_on_id":"br-6tzh9","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:05:03.329832480Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-91gti","depends_on_id":"br-7kigk","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:11:27.924032425Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-91gti","depends_on_id":"br-kpvhy","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:05:02.898819586Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-91gti","depends_on_id":"br-t540i","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:11:28.196967876Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-91gti","depends_on_id":"br-tsqxo","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:11:28.415680461Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"br-9bmtl","title":"Attention Feed Phase 4b1: Build comprehensive unit and integration coverage for the new operator loop","description":"# Why this bead exists
The earlier test bead covered the core feed, but it still left too much room for user-facing summary surfaces to be under-tested. If dashboard/terse and profile behavior are part of the product contract, they need deterministic automated coverage, not just manual spot-checking.

# Scope
1. Add deterministic unit tests for envelope serialization, cursor allocation, replay windows, eviction, mapping, derived signals, snapshot bootstrap, digest coalescing, wait conditions, profile resolution, and summary/view-model generation.
2. Add integration tests for CLI and server parity so the same underlying event looks materially the same across command and transport surfaces.
3. Add deterministic tests for dashboard/terse summary output, including quiet, interesting, action-required, degraded, and profile-specific cases.
4. Add command-chain tests for snapshot -> events/digest/wait/attention handoff where a full transcript-style e2e harness is not required.
5. Keep this bead focused on deterministic unit/integration verification; long-form transcript scenarios live in the paired e2e bead.

# Logging / artifact expectations
- Failed tests should log cursor ranges, matched conditions, suppression decisions, active profiles, transport mode, and summary bucket state.
- Golden/parity tests should emit diff-friendly diagnostics when payloads or terse/dashboard summaries drift.
- Integration tests should capture enough structured context to explain parity failures without immediate manual reproduction.
- Any shared summary/view-model regressions should print both the underlying signal snapshot and the rendered summary so future maintainers can see where divergence started.

# Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Core feed, snapshot bootstrap, digest, wait, attention, transport, profile, and dashboard/terse summary semantics are covered automatically at unit/integration level.
- [ ] Important failure modes such as expired cursor, unsupported replay window, partial observability, and contradictory profile/filter combinations have explicit automated coverage.
- [ ] Logs are detailed enough to debug failures without guesswork.

# Test commands that must pass before closing this bead
- `go build ./cmd/ntm`
- `go test -short ./...`
- package-targeted tests for `internal/robot/...`, `internal/serve/...`, `internal/events/...`, `internal/tracker/...`, and any dashboard/output packages touched by the feature","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":240,"created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:21.798002184Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-21T01:12:36.121519215Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["attention-feed","phase-4","robot","tests","verification"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-9bmtl","depends_on_id":"br-6tzh9","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:29.031845838Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-9bmtl","depends_on_id":"br-7kigk","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:29.394483660Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-9bmtl","depends_on_id":"br-91gti","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:11:29.447157567Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-9bmtl","depends_on_id":"br-auag6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:11:29.710446136Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-9bmtl","depends_on_id":"br-slg9g","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T00:57:17.871290905Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-9bmtl","depends_on_id":"br-t540i","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:29.761115149Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-9bmtl","depends_on_id":"br-tsqxo","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:28.667513894Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"br-a1ti3","title":"Overlay-Feed T7: Verify end-to-end overlay-feed integration and document invariants","description":"# Why this bead exists
The overlay/feed branch needs its own final verification gate for the same reason the core attention-feed initiative does: the question is not whether each sub-bead sounds plausible, but whether the whole human/agent handoff loop actually works coherently and leaves behind understandable artifacts for future maintainers.

# Scope
1. Verify the overlay/feed cold-start path when the feed is unavailable or empty.
2. Verify the warm path where real attention items, badge state, and overlay summaries agree.
3. Verify zoom/dismiss continuity events and cursor hints.
4. Verify cursor-targeted human handoff from an agent-facing attention response into the overlay.
5. Verify `assign --watch` workflow discoverability and overlay entry.
6. Confirm all documented invariants and non-goals remain true.
7. Confirm the overlay branch still degrades honestly when the feed is unavailable.
8. Leave behind a concise record of what commands/scenarios were run and where the most useful artifacts live.

# Invariants that must hold
- The overlay never becomes a second event source of truth.
- Human-originated overlay events remain background-level continuity signals, not synthetic emergencies.
- The feed can function without the overlay, and the overlay can degrade gracefully without the feed.
- Cursor identity used in overlay handoff remains the same cursor identity used by the attention feed.

# Detailed verification expectations
- Transcript-style e2e scenarios for cold start, warm path, zoom-return loop, agent handoff, watch-mode integration, and degraded/no-feed behavior.
- TUI or screenshot artifacts where they materially help future debugging.
- Diff-friendly diagnostics that identify which invariant failed when a scenario breaks.
- Logs that record cursor values, selected focus targets, emitted human-originated events, overlay state, and degraded-mode markers.

# Acceptance criteria
- [ ] The overlay/feed integration behaves coherently across its main happy paths and degraded paths.
- [ ] The documented invariants are verified, not just asserted.
- [ ] Useful artifacts remain behind for future debugging.
- [ ] Overlay/feed integration remains subordinate to the core attention-feed contract rather than forking it.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:09:03.851325660Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-21T01:22:21.713555773Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["attention-feed","gate","overlay","phase-3","verification"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-a1ti3","depends_on_id":"br-5u235","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:10:31.515583773Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-a1ti3","depends_on_id":"br-a6cmp","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:10:35.079137474Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-a1ti3","depends_on_id":"br-abp0s","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:10:32.921699049Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-a1ti3","depends_on_id":"br-ht8jy","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:10:33.661958365Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-a1ti3","depends_on_id":"br-la5zy","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:10:34.921653572Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-a1ti3","depends_on_id":"br-rb9oj","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:16:15.468849274Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-a1ti3","depends_on_id":"br-ut0d8","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:10:32.761963761Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-a1ti3","depends_on_id":"br-x66gc","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:10:41.208284760Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-a1ti3","depends_on_id":"br-xq4b0","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:16:15.141183759Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"br-a6cmp","title":"Overlay-Feed T6: Add --robot-overlay command for agent-initiated human handoff","description":"# Why this bead exists
The attention feed is the sensor side of the operator loop. Sometimes the correct actuator response is not "send another prompt" but "bring the human to the right place." This bead gives robot-mode a structured overlay handoff surface so an agent can do that mechanically instead of resorting to ad hoc human instructions.

# Scope
1. Add a robot-mode command for opening the overlay for a target session with structured JSON success/error behavior.
2. Support an optional attention cursor so the robot command can open the overlay pre-focused on the relevant attention item.
3. Follow existing robot conventions for exit codes, `success`, `timestamp`, and structured errors.
4. Be explicit about whether the command blocks until dismissal or supports a no-wait path; whichever behavior wins must be documented and testable.
5. Keep the command a thin actuator layer over existing overlay functionality rather than inventing another orchestration surface.

# Design considerations
- The command should make human handoff possible without pretending ntm can decide when a human should be involved.
- Blocking versus non-blocking behavior matters operationally and must not be left implicit.
- Failure cases such as not being inside tmux or missing session state must be machine-usable and honest.

# Detailed verification expectations
- Golden tests for success, unavailable, and failure JSON payloads.
- Tests for blocking/no-wait behavior and structured exit-code conventions.
- Integration tests covering cursor-targeted overlay handoff via the T4 surface.
- Transcript scenarios where an agent wakes on attention, decides to involve a human, opens the overlay, and records the resulting handoff artifacts.
- Detailed logs in tests that print session, cursor, blocking mode, emitted JSON, and failure reason.

# Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Robot-mode can open the overlay through a structured, machine-usable command.
- [ ] Cursor-targeted handoff is supported once the overlay focus surface exists.
- [ ] Blocking behavior and error semantics are explicit and tested.
- [ ] Automated tests and transcript artifacts make human-handoff behavior auditable.","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:08:39.758756568Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-21T01:15:56.500426883Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["attention-feed","cli","overlay","phase-3","robot"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-a6cmp","depends_on_id":"br-ht8jy","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:10:49.821416803Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-a6cmp","depends_on_id":"br-x66gc","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:10:40.039354013Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"br-phdna","title":"EPIC: TUI Glamour Upgrade — Leverage Full Charmbracelet Ecosystem","description":"# TUI Glamour Upgrade Epic

## Executive Summary
NTM's TUI (49,213 LOC) is a sophisticated Bubble Tea application with 22 dashboard panels,
3 Catppuccin themes, responsive 5-tier layout, and custom animations. However, it dramatically
underutilizes the Charmbracelet ecosystem it depends on. The `harmonica` spring physics library
is in go.mod with ZERO imports. `bubbles/table`, `bubbles/viewport`, `bubbles/progress`,
`bubbles/spinner`, `bubbles/help`, and `bubbles/list` are all available but replaced by
hand-rolled alternatives. Mouse support is never enabled. The `huh` forms library is absent.

This epic systematically upgrades the TUI to leverage these battle-tested components, adds
spring-based physics animations via Harmonica, enables mouse interaction, and decomposes the
7,017-line dashboard.go monolith — all verified at every step via the TUI Inspector VHS
capture-and-compare loop.

## Architecture Context
- Entry: `cmd/ntm/main.go` → `cli.Execute()` → Cobra commands
- Dashboard: `internal/tui/dashboard/dashboard.go` (7,017 LOC monolith)
- Components: `internal/tui/components/` (15 files, ~3,300 LOC)
- Styling: `internal/tui/styles/` + `internal/tui/theme/` (~3,000 LOC)
- Layout: `internal/tui/layout/layout.go` (5 responsive tiers with hysteresis)
- Panels: `internal/tui/dashboard/panels/` (22 implementations)
- TUI programs: dashboard, palette (cli/palette.go), tutorial (cli/tutorial.go), init_wizard

## Key Dependencies Already in go.mod
- charmbracelet/bubbletea v1.3.10
- charmbracelet/bubbles v1.0.0 (table, list, viewport, progress, spinner, help — all unused)
- charmbracelet/lipgloss v1.1.1-0.20250404203927-76690c660834
- charmbracelet/harmonica v0.2.0 (ZERO imports anywhere)
- charmbracelet/glamour v0.10.0
- charmbracelet/x/ansi v0.11.6

## Verification Infrastructure
- VHS installed at /home/ubuntu/go/bin/vhs
- 5 NTM-specific TUI Inspector profiles: ntm-dashboard-empty, ntm-dashboard-help,
  ntm-dashboard-navigate, ntm-dashboard-wide, ntm-palette
- Capture script: /dp/tui_inspector/scripts/capture_ntm_tui.sh
- Suite runner: /dp/tui_inspector/scripts/run_ntm_tui_suite.sh
- 5 native VHS tapes in testdata/vhs/ for unit-level visual regression

## Overarching Goals
1. Replace hand-rolled components with battle-tested Bubbles equivalents
2. Add spring-based physics animations via Harmonica (already a dependency!)
3. Enable mouse interaction for modern terminal UX
4. Decompose the dashboard.go monolith for maintainability
5. Add Huh forms for interactive workflows
6. Verify every change via TUI Inspector capture → compare → iterate loop
7. Maintain zero regressions in existing functionality","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-03-20T22:24:27.002836528Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-21T08:55:33.392668886Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["charmbracelet","epic","tui","upgrade"]}
 {"id":"br-pihrr","title":"Phase 2b: Add bubbles/table to History and Timeline Panels","description":"# Add bubbles/table to History and Timeline Panels

## Background
panels/history.go (862 LOC) renders tabular data with manual string formatting AND has
3 filter systems: historyStatusFilter (all/ok/error), historyAgentFilter (all/claude/codex/
gemini), historyTimeFilter (all/1h/24h/7d). It also has cursor management, replay on Enter,
and keybindings for filter cycling.

panels/timeline.go (1,111 LOC) similarly renders events with agent colors.

bubbles/table provides column alignment, scrolling, selection, and mouse support — but we
must PRESERVE the existing filter systems and replay functionality.

## CRITICAL: Existing Filter Architecture
  historyStatusFilter: Cycle with 'f' key → all/ok/error
  historyAgentFilter: Cycle with 'a' key → all/claude/codex/gemini
  historyTimeFilter: Cycle with 't' key → all/1h/24h/7d
  applyFilters(): Combines all 3 filters → produces filtered entry list
  moveCursor(): Manages selection within filtered results

bubbles/table does NOT have built-in filtering. The filter logic STAYS in the panel.
Only the RENDERING of filtered results moves to bubbles/table.

## Implementation Plan — History Panel

### 1. Add table.Model to HistoryPanel struct
  type HistoryPanel struct {
      PanelBase
      table    table.Model        // NEW: replaces inline rendering
      entries  []history.Entry    // raw data
      filtered []history.Entry    // after filters applied
      // ... existing filter fields preserved
  }

### 2. Define columns (adaptive to tier)
  func historyColumns(width int) []table.Column {
      if width < 120 { return narrowColumns() }
      if width < 200 { return splitColumns() }
      return wideColumns()
  }

### 3. Convert filtered entries → table.Row on each filter change
  func (m *HistoryPanel) rebuildTableRows() {
      rows := make([]table.Row, len(m.filtered))
      for i, e := range m.filtered {
          rows[i] = table.Row{formatTime(e.Timestamp), e.Command, e.Target, statusBadge(e.Status)}
      }
      m.table.SetRows(rows)
  }

### 4. Filter cycling preserved — after filter change, call rebuildTableRows()
### 5. Replay on Enter — get selected row index, map back to filtered[index]
### 6. Style with Catppuccin theme
### 7. Route table.Update(msg) when panel focused

## Timeline Panel — Similar approach
  Columns: Time, Event Type, Agent, Description
  Agent column uses AgentBadge for color coding
  No filter system (simpler than history)

## Unit Tests (REQUIRED)

### Expand existing: panels/history_test.go (634 LOC)
  func TestHistoryTableColumns(t *testing.T) {
      p := NewHistoryPanel()
      p.SetSize(120, 20)
      p.SetEntries(testHistoryEntries(10), nil)
      view := p.View()
      if !strings.Contains(view, "Time") { t.Error("missing Time header") }
      if !strings.Contains(view, "Command") { t.Error("missing Command header") }
  }

  func TestHistoryTableNavigation(t *testing.T) {
      p := NewHistoryPanel()
      p.SetSize(120, 20)
      p.SetEntries(testHistoryEntries(5), nil)
      p.Focus()
      p.Update(tea.KeyMsg{Type: tea.KeyDown})
      sel, ok := p.selectedEntry()
      if !ok { t.Error("should have selection after down") }
      _ = sel
  }

  func TestHistoryTableFilterPreserved(t *testing.T) {
      p := NewHistoryPanel()
      p.SetSize(120, 20)
      p.SetEntries(testHistoryEntries(10), nil) // mix of ok and error
      // Apply status filter
      p.Update(tea.KeyMsg{Type: tea.KeyRunes, Runes: []rune{'f'}}) // cycle filter
      // Verify table rows reflect filter
      // Table should have fewer rows than total entries
  }

  func TestHistoryTableTierAdaptation(t *testing.T) {
      p := NewHistoryPanel()
      p.SetSize(100, 20) // Narrow
      view := p.View()
      // Narrow columns should be used
      p.SetSize(250, 20) // Wide
      view = p.View()
      // Wide columns should include Target
  }

  func TestHistoryTableEmptyState(t *testing.T) {
      p := NewHistoryPanel()
      p.SetSize(120, 20)
      p.SetEntries(nil, nil)
      view := p.View()
      if view == "" { t.Error("empty state should render") }
  }

  func TestHistoryTableScrolling(t *testing.T) {
      p := NewHistoryPanel()
      p.SetSize(120, 10)
      p.SetEntries(testHistoryEntries(50), nil)
      p.Focus()
      for i := 0; i < 20; i++ { p.Update(tea.KeyMsg{Type: tea.KeyDown}) }
  }

### Expand existing: panels/timeline_test.go (1039 LOC)
  func TestTimelineTableColumns(t *testing.T) { ... }
  func TestTimelineTableAgentBadge(t *testing.T) { ... }

### Benchmark
  func BenchmarkHistoryTableView(b *testing.B) {
      p := NewHistoryPanel()
      p.SetSize(120, 20)
      p.SetEntries(testHistoryEntries(100), nil)
      b.ResetTimer()
      for i := 0; i < b.N; i++ { _ = p.View() }
  }

## E2E / VHS Verification
Create testdata/vhs/dashboard-table-scroll.tape. Tab to history panel, arrow down, PgDown.
Run TUI Inspector ntm-dashboard-navigate profile.

## Logging
- Log table rebuild: "history table: %d rows (filtered from %d)"
- Log column adaptation: "history table: %d cols for width=%d"
- Debug log filter changes: "history: filter %s -> %s"

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] History panel uses bubbles/table for rendering
- [ ] 3 filter systems (status/agent/time) preserved and functional
- [ ] Filter cycling ('f'/'a'/'t' keys) works and rebuilds table rows
- [ ] Replay on Enter works via filtered entry index
- [ ] Column widths adapt to layout tier
- [ ] Empty state renders gracefully
- [ ] Catppuccin styling on header/selected/cells
- [ ] Timeline panel uses bubbles/table with AgentBadge column
- [ ] All existing history_test.go (634 LOC) tests pass
- [ ] All existing timeline_test.go (1039 LOC) tests pass
- [ ] 6+ new tests pass per panel
- [ ] VHS shows proper column alignment and scrolling

## Files to Modify
- internal/tui/dashboard/panels/history.go (add table.Model, rebuildTableRows)
- internal/tui/dashboard/panels/timeline.go (add table.Model)

## Files to Create
None — tests go in existing history_test.go and timeline_test.go

## Risk: MEDIUM — Filter-to-table data flow is the key complexity. Replay must correctly
map table selection index back to the filtered entry. Test filter+replay combination.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":240,"created_at":"2026-03-20T22:28:14.484246692Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-21T03:31:49.299269008Z","closed_at":"2026-03-21T03:31:49.298229653Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["bubbles","phase-2","table","tui","upgrade"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-pihrr","depends_on_id":"br-bgfdt","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-20T22:33:06.562548860Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-pihrr","depends_on_id":"br-phdna","type":"related","created_at":"2026-03-20T22:34:49.968470577Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"br-rb9oj","title":"Overlay-Feed T9: Integration tests and Go e2e scenarios with artifact logging for overlay-feed interaction","description":"# Why this bead exists
Once the attention feed is operational and the overlay integration beads are implemented, we need integration and end-to-end verification that proves the human/agent handoff loop actually works. The earlier version of this bead planned shell-script e2e drivers, which is a poor fit for this Go-only repo. This bead now standardizes on Go-based integration and e2e harnesses with detailed artifact logging.

# Scope
1. Add in-process Go integration tests for overlay/feed interaction, including:
   - attention panel data flow and truncation
   - badge state transitions
   - cursor propagation through zoom/dismiss continuity paths
   - attention-cursor startup/focus behavior
   - graceful degradation when feed visibility is unavailable
2. Add Go-based e2e scenarios under `e2e/` that drive real tmux sessions for:
   - overlay launch and dismiss
   - auto-popup detection
   - overlay binding setup and cleanup
   - zoom-to-pane with cursor hint propagation
   - `assign --watch` overlay discoverability
   - robot-initiated overlay handoff once available
3. Make the Go e2e harness idempotent and self-cleaning so it creates and tears down its own tmux sessions/resources.
4. Ensure all scenarios use the shared Go scenario/artifact harness instead of inventing separate fixture and logging conventions.
5. Ensure all scenarios produce detailed, timestamped artifacts in predictable locations inside the repo's test artifact structure or an explicitly documented temporary artifact root.
6. Keep graceful degradation scenarios first-class rather than treating them as an afterthought.
7. Use Go test code as the canonical harness. Auxiliary debugging helpers are fine, but not shell-script primary runners.

# Detailed verification expectations
- Subtest-based integration coverage with `t.Logf` diagnostics for state transitions, cursor propagation, and rendered output.
- Go e2e scenarios with timeout protection, explicit cleanup, and artifact capture for stdout/stderr, key state transitions, and session lifecycle.
- Artifact logs should include timestamps, session identifiers, cursor values, focus targets, and degraded-mode markers where relevant.
- Failures should report enough structured context that a maintainer can understand what broke without immediately reproducing it interactively.

# Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Integration coverage exists for the key overlay/feed interaction paths and graceful degradation.
- [ ] Go-based e2e scenarios exist for the main overlay/feed workflows and clean up their own resources.
- [ ] The scenarios reuse the shared Go scenario/artifact harness rather than duplicating fixture plumbing.
- [ ] Detailed timestamped artifacts are produced in predictable locations.
- [ ] The primary verification harness is implemented in Go rather than shell scripts.
- [ ] The bead preserves all previously planned happy-path and degraded-path coverage.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:14:52.872086749Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-21T01:25:35.949568520Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["attention-feed","e2e","overlay","phase-3","tests","verification"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-rb9oj","depends_on_id":"br-5u235","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:16:08.534278552Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-rb9oj","depends_on_id":"br-7746h","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:25:31.544052650Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-rb9oj","depends_on_id":"br-7h8it","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:25:31.803751872Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-rb9oj","depends_on_id":"br-a6cmp","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:16:13.562886901Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-rb9oj","depends_on_id":"br-abp0s","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:16:10.690138392Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-rb9oj","depends_on_id":"br-ht8jy","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:16:11.494253800Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-rb9oj","depends_on_id":"br-la5zy","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:16:12.350680821Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-rb9oj","depends_on_id":"br-ut0d8","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:16:09.720207583Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-rb9oj","depends_on_id":"br-x66gc","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:16:14.447924217Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-rb9oj","depends_on_id":"br-xq4b0","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:16:07.739874585Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
-{"id":"br-rkh17","title":"Attention Feed Phase 4a1: Document the operator loop in robot help and capabilities output","description":"# Why this bead exists
Help and capabilities output are the canonical contract surface for both human users and agent drivers. They should not be bundled with dashboard rendering concerns. This bead isolates the machine-readable and CLI-discoverability side of the work.

# Scope
1. Update `--robot-help` and `--robot-capabilities` so the attention-feed command cluster, condition taxonomy, cursor/resync behavior, and profile/preset system are explicitly documented.
2. Include concrete examples for the canonical operator loop: snapshot bootstrap, steady-state attention/digest, raw event replay, and expired-cursor resync.
3. Make unsupported or partially observable conditions explicit in capabilities output, especially for `bead_orphaned` if it remains unavailable.
4. Ensure examples and schema notes are accurate enough that future users do not need a separate markdown plan document.
5. Keep the output aligned with actual implementation rather than aspirational language.
6. Expose a machine-readable support matrix covering commands, conditions, profiles, transport features, degraded/unsupported reasons, and any schema/capability version markers needed by agent clients.
7. Document cursor-retention and transport-liveness expectations strongly enough that long-lived clients can adapt without trial and error.

# Design considerations
- Help/capabilities are part of the product surface, not an afterthought.
- Capability output should be precise enough for an agent to adapt automatically.
- The text should emphasize the one-obvious-way operator loop rather than listing an undifferentiated pile of commands.
- Machine-readable capability output should tell agents not just what exists, but which features are supported, degraded, or intentionally unavailable and why.

# Likely touch points
- `internal/cli/root.go`
- capability/help generation in robot-mode code
- `internal/output/`

# Detailed verification expectations
- Golden tests for help/capabilities output.
- Tests proving the documented conditions, profiles, commands, schema-version markers, and transport-feature support matrix match implementation reality.
- Diff-friendly diagnostics when examples or capability flags drift.
- Scenario checks that help/capabilities mention snapshot bootstrap, resync, retention hints, and transport-liveness expectations explicitly.

# Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Help and capabilities output accurately teach the operator loop.
- [ ] Conditions, profiles, transport features, and unsupported/degraded behavior are represented honestly.
- [ ] Capability output is machine-readable enough for agent clients to adapt automatically.
- [ ] The contract is discoverable from ntm itself without external documents.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":180,"created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:21.584305811Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-21T01:25:34.736588130Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["attention-feed","dashboard","docs","phase-4","robot"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-rkh17","depends_on_id":"br-6tzh9","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:27.565570681Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-rkh17","depends_on_id":"br-7kigk","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:27.950976700Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-rkh17","depends_on_id":"br-91gti","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:11:28.696168421Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-rkh17","depends_on_id":"br-kpvhy","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:26.873210361Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-rkh17","depends_on_id":"br-slg9g","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T00:57:17.501881264Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-rkh17","depends_on_id":"br-t540i","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:28.315242931Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-rkh17","depends_on_id":"br-tsqxo","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:27.204026005Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
+{"id":"br-rkh17","title":"Attention Feed Phase 4a1: Document the operator loop in robot help and capabilities output","description":"# Why this bead exists

2. Include concrete examples for the canonical operator loop: snapshot bootstrap, steady-state attention/digest, raw event replay, and expired-cursor resync.
3. Make unsupported or partially observable conditions explicit in capabilities output, especially for `bead_orphaned` if it remains unavailable.
4. Ensure examples and schema notes are accurate enough that future users do not need a separate markdown plan document.
5. Keep the output aligned with actual implementation rather than aspirational language.
6. Expose a machine-readable support matrix covering commands, conditions, profiles, transport features, degraded/unsupported reasons, and any schema/capability version markers needed by agent clients.
7. Document cursor-retention and transport-liveness expectations strongly enough that long-lived clients can adapt without trial and error.

# Design considerations
- Help/capabilities are part of the product surface, not an afterthought.
- Capability output should be precise enough for an agent to adapt automatically.
- The text should emphasize the one-obvious-way operator loop rather than listing an undifferentiated pile of commands.
- Machine-readable capability output should tell agents not just what exists, but which features are supported, degraded, or intentionally unavailable and why.

# Likely touch points
- `internal/cli/root.go`
- capability/help generation in robot-mode code
- `internal/output/`

# Detailed verification expectations
- Golden tests for help/capabilities output.
- Tests proving the documented conditions, profiles, commands, schema-version markers, and transport-feature support matrix match implementation reality.

# Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Help and capabilities output accurately teach the operator loop.
- [ ] Conditions, profiles, transport features, and unsupported/degraded behavior are represented honestly.
- [ ] Capability output is machine-readable enough for agent clients to adapt automatically.
- [ ] The contract is discoverable from ntm itself without external documents.","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":180,"created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:21.584305811Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-21T09:08:09.636203680Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["attention-feed","dashboard","docs","phase-4","robot"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-rkh17","depends_on_id":"br-6tzh9","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:27.565570681Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-rkh17","depends_on_id":"br-7kigk","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:27.950976700Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-rkh17","depends_on_id":"br-91gti","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:11:28.696168421Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-rkh17","depends_on_id":"br-kpvhy","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:26.873210361Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-rkh17","depends_on_id":"br-slg9g","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T00:57:17.501881264Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-rkh17","depends_on_id":"br-t540i","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:28.315242931Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-rkh17","depends_on_id":"br-tsqxo","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:27.204026005Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"br-slg9g","title":"Attention Feed Phase 2a2: Add snapshot attention summary and bootstrap ergonomics","description":"# Why this bead exists
Once snapshot bootstrap correctness exists, snapshot still needs to be genuinely useful to operators. A raw cold-start dump with a cursor is functional, but not ergonomic. This bead adds the snapshot-level attention summary and bootstrap UX polish that helps a frontier LLM orient quickly without turning snapshot into a second digest implementation.

# Scope
1. Add a compact snapshot-level attention summary aligned with the same actionability/severity taxonomy used by the feed and digest surfaces.
2. Surface top attention items or compact counts in a way that helps an operator choose the next command without rereading the entire snapshot.
3. Ensure any snapshot summary stays consistent with the real signal set: core signals, mail signals, conflict signals, and honest unsupported-condition behavior.
4. Add resync guidance and next-step hints that are mechanical rather than speculative.
5. Keep snapshot summary logic shallow enough that digest remains the canonical steady-state delta surface.

# Design considerations
- Snapshot should help an operator orient, not replace the digest.
- Summary counts and hints must be generated from the same underlying signal taxonomy so snapshot/digest do not drift apart.
- Unsupported or partially observable conditions must be represented honestly, not smoothed over.

# Likely touch points
- `internal/robot/robot.go`
- snapshot-producing helpers
- `internal/output/`
- help/capabilities examples that describe bootstrap flow

# Detailed verification expectations
- Golden tests for snapshot responses that include attention summary fields.
- Tests that compare snapshot summary buckets against the underlying signal state for consistency.
- Transcript scenarios showing a realistic cold-start operator loop using the summary to choose the next targeted command.
- Detailed logs in tests for summary bucket counts, unsupported-condition markers, and next-step hints.

# Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Snapshot surfaces a compact operator-oriented summary without duplicating digest semantics.
- [ ] Snapshot summary is derived from the same real signal taxonomy as the rest of the attention-feed system.
- [ ] Unsupported or partial conditions are represented honestly.
- [ ] Bootstrap ergonomics are materially better for users while preserving the cold-start contract.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":150,"created_at":"2026-03-21T00:57:12.030218053Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-21T08:47:46.142142953Z","closed_at":"2026-03-21T08:47:46.141073029Z","close_reason":"SnapshotAttentionSummary type and buildSnapshotAttentionSummary function added. Snapshot now includes compact orientation with action-required counts, top items (capped at 3), category breakdown, unsupported signals, and mechanical next-step hints. Tests pass.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["attention-feed","bootstrap","cli","phase-2","robot","snapshot"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-slg9g","depends_on_id":"br-04qv0","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:01:37.920524355Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-slg9g","depends_on_id":"br-3qs46","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:01:39.045717977Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-slg9g","depends_on_id":"br-89wvt","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:01:37.548558448Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-slg9g","depends_on_id":"br-fsoxa","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:01:38.287606898Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-slg9g","depends_on_id":"br-vdfjr","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:01:38.660610688Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"br-t540i","title":"Attention Feed Phase 3d: Add --robot-attention as the one obvious tending primitive","description":"# Why this bead exists
An agent-friendly system should have one obvious tending primitive, not five equally plausible loops. This bead adds `--robot-attention` as the recommended macro for operator agents: sleep until attention is needed, then wake with a compact digest.

# Scope
1. Implement `--robot-attention` as a transparent composition of wait + digest over the same cursor model.
2. Return the wake reason / matched condition alongside the digest so the operator knows why it woke up.
3. Preserve full transparency: no hidden planning, no hidden state machine, no magic side effects.
4. Make cursor handoff obvious so a tending loop can call the command repeatedly without bespoke bookkeeping.
5. Ensure the command works cleanly starting from a snapshot-provided cursor and after an expired-cursor resync.

# Design considerations
- This should be the one command a fungible tending agent can memorize first.
- The command should reduce token burn, not add ceremony.
- It must remain a convenience surface, not a new coordinator.

# Detailed verification expectations
- Tests for wake-on-attention behavior.
- Tests for cursor chaining across repeated calls.
- Golden tests for the combined response shape.
- Transcript-style looping scenario that runs snapshot -> attention -> targeted follow-up -> attention again, with a simulated replay-expiration/resync hop.
- Detailed logs for wake reason, returned digest summary, and next cursor when test expectations fail.

# Acceptance criteria
- [ ] `--robot-attention` exists and clearly behaves like wait-then-digest.
- [ ] Wake reason / matched condition is explicit in the response.
- [ ] Cursor chaining is obvious enough for a long-lived tending loop.
- [ ] The command introduces no hidden orchestration state.
- [ ] Snapshot/resync interactions are covered by automated scenario tests.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":90,"created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:21.367086542Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-21T08:57:53.485259900Z","closed_at":"2026-03-21T08:57:53.484781915Z","close_reason":"PrintAttention implemented in events.go with wait+digest composition: AttentionOptions, AttentionResponse (WakeReason, MatchedCondition, TriggerEvent, Digest, CursorInfo with NextCommand for chaining), cursor expiration handling with resync, timeout support. Build passes.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["attention","attention-feed","cli","phase-3","robot"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-t540i","depends_on_id":"br-6tzh9","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:26.532342527Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-t540i","depends_on_id":"br-7kigk","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:26.188583019Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"br-tsqxo","title":"Attention Feed Phase 2c: Upgrade SSE, WebSocket, and HTTP transport to expose the same feed","description":"# Why this bead exists
The CLI cannot be the only way to consume the attention feed. ntm already has SSE, WebSocket, and HTTP surfaces, but they currently expose only a small subset of what an operator agent actually needs. This bead upgrades network transport so remote or embedded clients see the same feed contract instead of a watered-down variant.

# Scope
1. Upgrade the existing event streaming surfaces so they emit the normalized envelope rather than minimal type/timestamp/session stubs.
2. Add replay and filter support where appropriate so network clients can resume from a cursor instead of polling snapshots.
3. Preserve parity between CLI and network outputs; the same event should look materially the same regardless of transport.
4. Keep transport-specific wrappers thin. The feed contract should live in shared code, not in per-endpoint ad hoc shaping.
5. Make replay failure / cursor-expiration semantics as explicit over the network as they are in CLI mode.
6. Define explicit liveness behavior for long-lived transports: heartbeat/keepalive strategy, idle-period semantics, and reconnect guidance so clients can distinguish a quiet stream from a dead connection.
7. Ensure machine-readable capability/version output can tell remote clients which transport features are actually supported.

# Design considerations
- SSE and WebSocket differ operationally, but they should not invent different semantics.
- If replay/resume cannot be satisfied, the transport should return the same resync guidance the CLI gets.
- This bead should improve agent ergonomics for remote controllers without forcing them to shell out to the CLI.
- Heartbeats should be useful enough for liveness detection without creating pointless noise or a second event taxonomy.

# Detailed verification expectations
- Integration tests for SSE replay/subscribe semantics.
- Integration tests for WebSocket event shape, resume behavior, and idle-period/heartbeat behavior.
- Parity tests comparing CLI/network payload shape for the same underlying event.
- Go-based e2e scenarios that exercise HTTP/SSE/WebSocket flows and write detailed transcript logs for initial subscribe, replay, idle heartbeat, expiration, reconnect, and resync behavior.
- Failure logs that capture transport, cursor, filter set, liveness behavior, and payload summary when parity tests fail.

# Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Network clients can subscribe to the normalized feed with the same core fields and cursor semantics as CLI clients.
- [ ] Replay/resume behavior is explicit and consistent.
- [ ] Existing `/events` / WebSocket behavior is upgraded without creating a second schema family.
- [ ] Long-lived transport liveness/heartbeat semantics are explicit and tested.
- [ ] Network parity is demonstrated by automated tests and transcript logs.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":180,"created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:20.476009644Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-21T08:52:46.511073239Z","closed_at":"2026-03-21T08:52:46.509782812Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["attention-feed","phase-2","robot","server","streaming"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-tsqxo","depends_on_id":"br-04qv0","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:23.842024051Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-tsqxo","depends_on_id":"br-kpvhy","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:11:27.712423871Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

+// renderHelpOverlayBox renders a styled box with a title and content.
+// Uses the Catppuccin theme for consistent styling with the dashboard.
+func (m Model) renderHelpOverlayBox(title, content string, maxWidth int) string {
+	t := m.theme
+
+	// Title style
+	titleStyle := lipgloss.NewStyle().
+		Bold(true).
+		Foreground(t.Primary).
+		Padding(0, 1)
+
+	// Box style with rounded border
+	boxStyle := lipgloss.NewStyle().
+		Border(lipgloss.RoundedBorder()).
+		BorderForeground(t.Primary).
+		Background(t.Base).
+		Padding(1, 2).
+		Width(maxWidth)
+
+	// Combine title and content
+	fullContent := titleStyle.Render(title) + "\n\n" + content
+
+	return boxStyle.Render(fullContent)
+}
